summary: electron-orbit harvests environment variables at install and exfiltrates via obfuscated raw-TLS channel at import
details: The package's postinstall script (install.js, registered via package.json scripts.postinstall) enumerates process.env in bulk (filtering only keys containing 'path') and writes the collected variables to /tmp/electron_orbit_install_marker.txt. Access to process/env is XOR-obfuscated via an __s(key, bytes) decoder to evade string-based analysis. On import, the package main (dist/index.js) executes setupAutoRenderOnImport(), which in Node contexts reads that marker file, XOR-encodes it with the key 'electron-orbit', and stages it in memory as a hex blob, then invokes a hand-rolled TLS 1.3 client built directly on node:net (src/aetherls.ts) connecting to an XOR-obfuscated host on port 443 with no server-certificate validation. Activation of the network path is gated by SHA256(process.env.BuildType) matching a hardcoded digest, indicating targeted delivery. The postinstall additionally writes a fake 'native bootstrap' file under bin/formatters/ that prepends MZ/Mach-O/ELF magic bytes to a JSON payload containing os.hostname(), os.userInfo().username, node version, and platform — a cover story to justify the postinstall while collecting host identifiers. Package name and author ('electron-orbit', 'OrbitDB Archive', homepage github.com/orbitdb-archive/orbit-electron) impersonate the Electron and OrbitDB projects to earn install-on-sight trust. Installer harm: credentials present in the CI/developer environment (AWS_*, GITHUB_TOKEN, NPM_TOKEN, and any other non-'path' env keys) are captured at install and shipped off-host over a certificate-unvalidated channel at first import, and host identity is leaked alongside them.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
